target: A Wiki for Mizar: Motivation, Considerations, and Initial Prototype (The ﬁnal publication of this paper is available at www.springerlink.com)

The development of the Mizar Mathematical Library (mml), the principal, authoritative collection of Mizar contributions (called “articles”), has been the main activity of the Mizar project (http://mizar.org) since the late 1980s, as it has been believed within the project team that only substantial experience may help in improving (actually building) the system.
trailing: An article is presented as a text-file and contains theorems and definitions. (At the time of writing, there are 1073 articles in mml, containing 49548 facts/theorems and 9487 definitions.)

target: According to a recent listing (http://git.wiki.kernel.org/index.php/InterfacesFrontendsAndTools), there are today more than a dozen wikis based on the Git DVCS or using Git as a back-end.
trailing: Our design decision about wiki for Mizar is: the data (articles) in the Git repository should always be self-sufficient (independent of the wiki functionalities), and easily exchangable between various installations (that can be used just for local development, as a back-end for another wiki-like system, etc.).

target: As we discussed earlier, our prototype (see Figure 1) is initially focused on the Mizar developers who edit their work mainly offline and submit their changes to be viewed online (http://mws.cs.ru.nl/mwiki) by other developers.
trailing: The repository structure (and suggested future features) of our prototype is as follows.

preceding: There are good reasons to integrate realtime data sources both with static Web data and with each other, and Semantic Web technologies provide a potential platform for that integration.
target: For example, mapping real-time social data into common Semantic Web vocabularies [12][7] enables “smarter” realtime queries which draw upon the wealth of generalpurpose knowledge contained in the Linking Open Data cloud (http://esw.w3.org/SweoIG/TaskForces/CommunityProjects/LinkingOpenData).
trailing: Bridging the gap between sensor data and the symbolic space of the Semantic Web [4] opens the door to a semantic Internet of Things, while the combination of social network data with sensor data [3] promises more personalized and contextually aware real-time services.

preceding: Furthermore, various notions of RDF updates or changesets permit the communication of dynamic changes to that data, such as the posting of a photo or the change of a user’s geolocation.
target: Emerging technologies such as SPARQL 1.1 (http://www.w3.org/TR/2009/WD-sparql11-http-rdf-update-20091022/) and sparqlPuSH [10] provide transport mechanisms for updates.
trailing: As the Semantic Web moves into this new domain, then, performance and scalability issues should be kept in mind from the start.

target: The SPARQL/Update (http://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-update/) [11] language, nicknamed SPARUL, comes closest to a standard update language for RDF graphs.
trailing: Using a syntax derived from SPARQL, SPARUL provides several basic update operations, including statement-level insertion and deletion. See Figure 1 for an example.

target: Also worth mentioning are the very similar SparqlUpdateLanguage (http://esw.w3.org/SparqlUpdateLanguage) , as well the SPARQL update syntax of ARC’s SPARQL+ .

The Delta ontology[2] and Notation3 (http://www.w3.org/DesignIssues/Notation3( -based file format apply the notions of textual diff and patch to RDF graphs, permitting the syndication of changes to graphs distributed among two or more peers.
trailing: See Figure 2 for an example.

target: Changesets (http://n2.talis.com/wiki/Changesets) is a resource-oriented scheme for tracking changes to an RDF graph.
trailing: An update, or changeset, is centered on a single subject of change, such that the change is specific to the bnode closure, or concise bounded description, of that resource.

target: The Changeset RDF vocabulary (http://vocab.org/changeset/schema.html) uses RDF reification to express changes in terms of triples added or removed, and additionally includes terms to express meta-information about a change, including its time and purpose, the entity responsible, and the preceding change in a history of changes.
trailing: See Figure 3 for an example.

target: The Graph Update Ontology (GUO) (http://webr3.org/specs/guo/) defines an RDF diff in terms of triple-level insert and delete operations.
trailing: Like the Changesets vocabulary, GUO expresses an update as an RDF resource, allowing additional metadata to be attached to the update.

target: The Guaranteed RDF Update Format (GRUF) (http://websub.org/wiki/GRUF) is a proposed plain-text format for RDF updates.
trailing: While there are currently no software implementations of GRUF, it is more compact than any of the other formats described here, making it potentially appropriate for high-volume RDF update streams.

preceding: Statementlevel add and remove operations are expressed with subject-predicate-object triple patterns which include an optional named graph component.
target: The ease of parsing the simple XMLbased format efficiently has led to its reuse in the AllegroGraph triple store (http://www.franz.com/agraph/allegrograph/doc/new-http-server.html#header2-235), and consequently in the experimental evaluation described in Section 5.

preceding: Several other RDF update formats have been proposed, although they are closely tied with uncommon query languages.
target: The rdfDB Query Language (http://www.guha.com/rdfdb/query.html#insert), for example, provides insert and delete operations.
trailing: The Modification Exchange Language (MEL) [8] is based on RDF reification and interoperates with Edutella’s (http://www.edutella.org/edutella.shtml) Query Exchange Language (QEL).

preceding: The rdfDB Query Language, for example, provides insert and delete operations.

trailing: The RDF Update Language (RUL) [6] deals with type-safe class and property instance level updates, interoperating with the query and view languages RQL and RVL.

preceding: Furthermore, there are many additional technologies which deal with RDF updates and change notification, without however providing a statement-level update format.

preceding: Given the origins of the Semantic Web, is not surprising that nearly all of these protocols are based on HTTP.
target: The proposed XMPP bindings for the SPARQL protocol (http://danbri.org/words/2008/02/11/278) are an exception, while at a higher level, both HTTP and XMPP are usually layered upon the Transmission Control Protocol (TCP).
trailing: It is these lower levels of protocol which impose the most basic constraints on both the latency and throughput of RDF update streams sent over the Internet, so we will discuss them in the following, illustrating their well-known properties with small-scale experiments.

target: The framework described in this section (Source code for this study is available at: http:// fortytwo.net/research/rdfstream) is motivated by large-scale social networking services which generate more data than it is possible to transport between a single pair of widely separated Internet hosts.
trailing: Surprisingly, by giving up on transporting all of the data, we are in fact able to transport more data than would otherwise be possible.

target: Twitter’s Streaming API (http://dev.twitter.com/pages/streaming_api) provides near-real-time access to various subsets of public and protected Twitter data.
trailing: The most privileged level of access, the Firehose stream, contains some 500 to 1000 status updates, or “tweets” per second, where a tweet consists of a short snippet of text accompanied by several dozen fields of metadata including time and place of the update and a description of the author.

target: When translated into RDF using TwitLogic (http://twitlogic.fortytwo.net/), a tweet is represented with an average of 18 triples.
trailing: In order to update the triple store appropriately (for example, replacing the tweet author’s location with a new location), an average of 9 “remove” operations are also required, for a total of just under 30 update operations per tweet.

preceding: Finally, it is worth noting that the goal achieved in the preceding section – that of transporting, ingesting and querying over the Twitter Firehose – is a reasonably welldefined yet rather informal benchmark with respect to throughput of RDF data.
target: Much as concrete metrics such as the Lehigh University Benchmark (LUBM) (http://swat.cse.lehigh.edu/projects/lubm/) have been developed to evaluate RDF graph databases in terms of integrity and scalability, so there is also a need for metrics which address throughput and response time of highly dynamic real-time Semantic Web services.

Personally I don’t think that the fundamentals are changeable – the well established holographic principle (It even has a Wikipedia page which is not bad at http://en. wikipedia.org/wiki/Holographic_principle.) clearly shows that maximal entropy in any region scales with its radius squared and not cubed as might be expected [16] i.e. it is the boundary to a region which determines its maximum information content, not its volume.
trailing: Therefore, growth in storage capacity and the speed in accessing that storage will always diverge exponentially for exactly the same reason as why mass – or organisations, or anything which conserves information entropy – face decreasing marginal returns for additional investment into extra order.

target: OSLOM [http://www.oslom.org] [Lancichinetti et al. 2011] tests the statistical significance of a cluster [Bianconi et al. 2008] with respect to a global null model (i.e., the random graph generated by the configuration model [Molloy and Reed 1995]) during community expansion.
trailing: To grow the current community, the r value is computed for each neighbor, which is the cumulative probability of having the number of internal connections equal or larger than the number of connections from a neighbor into this community in the null model.

target: iLCD [http://cazabetremy.fr/Cazabet_remy/iLCD.html] [Cazabet et al. 2010] is capable of detecting both static and temporal communities.
trailing: Given a set of edges created at some time step, iLCD updates the existing communities by adding a new node if its number of second neighbors and number of robust second neighbors are greater than expected values.

target: Similar to EAGLE, GCE [https://sites.google.com/site/greedycliqueexpansion] [Lee et al. 2010] identifies maximum cliques as seed communities.
trailing: It expands these seeds by greedily optimizing a local fitness function.

preceding: In general, the number of mixture models is equal to the number of communities, which needs to be specified in advance.
target: In SPAEM [http://www.code.google.com/p/spaem] [Ren et al. 2009], the mixture model is viewed as a generative model for the links in the network.
trailing: Suppose that πr is the probability of observing community r and community r selects node i with probability Br,i.

preceding: As in [Kim and Jeong 2011], the optimal number of communities k is identified based on the minimum description length.
target: There is another algorithm called FOG [http://www.casos.cs.cmu.edu/projects/ora] [Davis and Carley 2008] also trying to infer groups based on link evidence.

preceding: Similar mixture models can also be constructed as a generative model for nodes [Fu and Banerjee 2008].
target: In SSDE [http://www.cs.rpi.edu/~purnej/code.php] [Magdon-ismail and Purnell 2011], the network is first mapped into a d-dimensional space using the spectral clustering method.
trailing: A Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) is then trained via Expectation-Maximization algorithm.

preceding: OSBM requires more efforts than mixture models because the factorization in the observed condition distribution for edges given Z is in general intractable.
target: MOSES [http://sites.google.com/site/aaronmcdaid/moses] [McDaid and Hurley 2010] combines OSBM with the local optimization scheme, in which the fitness function is defined based on the observed condition distribution.
trailing: MOSES greedily expands a community from edges.

preceding: In [Zhao et al. 2010], redundant constraints in the approximation are removed, reducing NMF to a problem of symmetrical non-negative matrix factorization (s-NMF).
target: Psorakis [Psorakis et al. 2011] proposed a hybrid algorithm called Bayesian NMF [http://www.robots.ox.ac.uk/~parg/software.html].
trailing: The matrix V, where each element vij denotes a count of the interactions that took place between two nodes i and j, is decomposed via NMF as part of the parameter inference for a generative model similar to OSBM and GMM.

preceding: The label propagation algorithm [Raghavan et al. 2007; Xie and Szymanski 2011], in which nodes with same label form a community, has been extended to overlapping community detection by allowing a node to have multiple labels.
target: In COPRA [http://www.cs.bris.ac.uk/~steve/networks/software/copra.html] [Gregory 2010], each node updates its belonging coefficients by averaging the coefficients from all its neighbors at each time step in a synchronous fashion.
trailing: The parameter v is used to control the maximum number of communities with which a node can associate.

target: SLPA [https://sites.google.com/site/communitydetectionslpa] [Xie et al. 2011; Xie and Szymanski 2012] is a general speaker-listener based information propagation process.
trailing: It spreads labels between nodes according to pairwise interaction rules.

target: CONGA [http://www.cs.bris.ac.uk/~steve/networks/software/conga.html] [Gregory 2007] extends Girvan and Newman's divisive clustering algorithm (GN) [Girvan and Newman 2002] by allowing a node to split into multiple copies.
trailing: Both splitting betweenness, defined by the number of shortest paths on the imaginary edge, and the conventional edge betweenness are considered.

target: Zhang [http://dbgroup.cs.tsinghua.edu.cn/zhangyz/kdd09] [Zhang et al. 2009] proposed an iterative process that reinforces the network topology and propinquity that is interpreted as the probability of a pair of nodes belonging to the same community.
trailing: The propinquity between two vertices is defined as the sum of the number of direct links, number of common neighbors and the number of links within the common neighborhood.

target: The LFR [http://sites.google.com/site/andrealancichinetti/files] benchmark proposed in [Lancichinetti et al. 2008] introduces heterogeneity into degree and community size distributions of a network.
trailing: These distributions are governed by power laws with exponents τ1 and τ2, respectively.

preceding: We used networks with sizes n ∈ {1000,5000}.
target: The average degree is kept at k = 10, which is of the same order as most large real-world social networks [snap.stanford.edu/data].
trailing: The rest of the parameters of LFR generator are set similar to those in [Lancichinetti and Fortunato 2009]: node degrees and community sizes are governed by power law distributions with exponents τ1 = 2 and τ2 = 1 respectively, the maximum degree is kmax = 50, and community sizes vary in both small range s = (10, 50) and large range b = (20, 100).

preceding: Next, we tested on a wider range of social networks listed in Table VII.

target: In this paper, we present a new catalogue of 5,106 IR bubbles identified via visual inspection of the GLIMPSE and MIPSGAL survey images as part of the citizen science Milky Way Project (http://www.milkywayproject.org/)(MWP)
trailing: Our catalogue expands the CP06 and CPW07 catalogues by nearly an order of magnitude and hence represents a far more complete sample of Galactic H ii regions.

preceding: One issue when assessing the quality of SKOS data is the so-called “Open World Assumption”, which underlies the Web of Data itself. Established quality notions from closed-world systems, such as referential integrity or schema validation, do not hold anymore, because available information may be incomplete and non-explicitly stated facts cannot be determined as true or false.
target: Work-arounds, often ad-hoc, are thus currently used to evaluate quality in Linked Data sets [7], as is done in the (rule-based) SKOS tools mentioned above, or the Pellet ICV (http://clarkparsia.com/pellet/icv/) , which re-interprets OWL axioms with integrity constraint semantics.

preceding: Literals should be tagged consistently [23], because omitting language tags or using nonstandardized, private language tags in a SKOS vocabulary could unintentionally limit the result set of language-dependent queries.

The list of 885 theorems proved only with advice is available online (http://mws.cs.ru.nl/~mptp/hh1/ATPdeps/aonly_by_length.txt) sorted by the number of necessary premises.
trailing: The last theorem in this order (CROSS_BASIS_- NONZERO) 38 used 34 premises for the advised ATP proof, while its HOL Light proof is just a single invocation of the VEC3_TAC tactic39 (which however brings in 121 HOL Light dependencies, making re-proving difficult).

preceding: The list of 885 theorems proved only with advice is available online37 sorted by the number of necessary premises.
target: The last theorem in this order (CROSS_BASIS_- NONZERO) (http://mws.cs.ru.nl/~mptp/cgi-bin/browseproofs.cgi?refs=CROSS_BASIS_NONZERO) used 34 premises for the advised ATP proof, while its HOL Light proof is just a single invocation of the VEC3_TAC tactic39 (which however brings in 121 HOL Light dependencies, making re-proving difficult).
trailing: The following two short examples show how the advice can sometimes get simpler proofs.

target: The HOL Light proof (http://mws.cs.ru.nl/~mptp/cgi-bin/browseproofs.cgi?refs=FACE_OF_POLYHEDRON_POLYHEDRON) takes 23 lines and could not be re-played by ATPs, but a much simpler proof was found by the AI/ATP automation, based on (a part of) the FACE_OF_STILLCONVEX theorem: a face t of any convex set s is equal to the intersection of s with the affine hull of t.
trailing: To finish the proof, one needs just three “obvious” facts: Every polyhedron is convex (POLYHEDRON_IMP_CONVEX), the intersection of two polyhedra is again a polyhedron (POLYHEDRON_INTER), and affine hull is always a polyhedron (POLYHEDRON_AFFINE_HULL):

target: The HOL Light proof (http://mws.cs.ru.nl/~mptp/cgi-bin/browseproofs.cgi?refs=FACE_OF_AFFINE_TRIVIAL) takes 19 lines and could not be re-played by ATPs.
trailing: The advised proof finds a simple path via previous theorem FACE_OF_DISJOINT_RELATIVE_- INTERIOR saying that nontrivial faces are disjoint with the relative interior, and theorem RELATIVE_INTERIOR_UNIV saying that any affine hull is equal to its relative interior.

target: Theorems COMPLEX_MUL_CNJ (http://mws.cs.ru.nl/~mptp/cgi-bin/browseproofs.cgi?refs=COMPLEX_MUL_CNJ) and COMPLEX_NORM_POW_2 stating the equality of squared norm to multiplication with a complex conjugate follow easily from each other (together with the commutativity of complex multiplication COMPLEX_MUL_- SYM).
trailing: The proof of COMPLEX_MUL_CNJ in HOL Light (below) re-uses the longer proof of COMPLEX_NORM_POW_2. The advised ATP proof directly uses COMPLEX_NORM_POW_2, but (likely because COMPLEX_MUL_SYM was never used before) first unfolds the definition of complex conjugate and then applies commutativity of real multiplication.

target: Theorem disjoint_line_interval (http://mws.cs.ru.nl/~mptp/cgi-bin/browseproofs.cgi?refs=Vol1.disjoint_line_interval) states that the left endpoints of two unitlong integer-ended intervals on the real line have to coincide if the intervals share a point strictly inside them.
trailing: This suggests case analysis, which is what the longer HOL Light proof (omitted here) seems to do.

target: Theorem NEGLIGIBLE_CONVEX_HULL_3 (http://mws.cs.ru.nl/~mptp/cgi-bin/browseproofs.cgi?refs=NEGLIGIBLE_CONVEX_HULL_3) states that the convex hull of three points in R 3 is a negligible set.
trailing: In HOL Light this is proved from the general theorem NEGLIGIBLE_CONVEX_HULL stating this property for any finite set of points in R n with cardinality less or equal to n.

target: Theorem BARV_CIRCUMCENTER_EXISTS (http://mws.cs.ru.nl/~mptp/cgi-bin/browseproofs.cgi?refs=Rogers.BARV_CIRCUMCENTER_EXISTS) says that under certain assumptions, a particular point (circumcenter) lies in a particular set (affine hull).
trailing: The HOL Light proof unfolds some of the assumptions and takes 14 lines.

target: An example of the reverse phenomenon (i.e., the advised proof is more complicated than the original) is theorem BOUNDED_CLOSURE_EQ (http://mws.cs.ru.nl/~mptp/cgi-bin/browseproofs.cgi?refs=BOUNDED_CLOSURE_EQ) saying that a set in R n is bounded iff its closure is bounded.
trailing: The harder direction of the equivalence was already available as theorem BOUNDED_CLOSURE, and was used both by the HOL Light and the advised proof.

preceding: Comparing the performance, our implementation exceeds the throughput of [7] by several orders of magnitude, and is for the first time able to scale to data sets of realistic size (exceeding the maximal amount considered in [7] by 10 orders of magnitude) for which the amount of extracted randomness actually exceeds the size of the initial seed, which marks the regime in which Trevisan's construction prevails over two-universal hashing.
target: Besides, the full source code of our implementation is available [1 The sources are available under the terms of the GNU General Public License (GPL), version 2— see www.gnu.org. Essentially, this means that the code can be used and modified free of charge for research (or even commercial) work, provided that any improvements to the code are made available under similar terms.] and can be inspected and used as basis for further research.
trailing: We therefore hope that our implementation will be of use for applications in the context of quantum cryptography, for implementing random number generators, or as a testbed for developing new ideas about extractors.

target: Links and URLs’ data are stored in a PostgreSQL (http://www.postgresql.org/) database.

preceding: Each crawler instance is only responsible for downloading and processing web pages.

preceding: Recently, general purpose computing on graphics processing units (GPGPU) has acquired considerable momentum in the scientific community.
target: This is confirmed both by increasing numbers of GPGPU-related publications and GPU-based supercomputers in the TOP500 (http://top500.org/) list.
trailing: Major programming frameworks are NVIDIA CUDA and OpenCL.

preceding: We look at both convenience and performance of the li-braries under consideration in the context of solving ordinary differential equations.
target: The comparison is based on odeint (http://odeint.com/), a modern C++ library for solving ordinary differential equations (ODEs) numerically [2, 3] which has been included into the Boost libraries3 recently.
trailing: It is developed in a generic way using template meta-programming techniques, which leads to extraordinary high flexibility at utmost performance.

preceding: The GPGPU libraries considered in this work are MTL4, VexCL, and ViennaCL.
target: We also employ Thrust (http://thrust.github.com/) in order to provide a reference point for the comparison of the considered libraries.
trailing: Thrust is a parallel algorithms library which resembles the C++ Standard Template Library [6].

target: MTL4 (The Matrix Template Library) (http://mtl4.org/) is a C++ linear algebra library providing an intuitive interface by establishing a domain-specific language embedded in C++ [12].
trailing: The library aims for maximal performance achievable by highlevel languages using compile-time transformations.

target: VexCL is a vector expression template library (https://github.com/ddemidov/vexcl) for OpenCL [11].
trailing: It has been created for ease of OpenCL development with C++.

target: ViennaCL (The Vienna Computing Library) is a scientific computing library (http://viennacl.sourceforge.net/) written in C++ [30].
trailing: CUDA and OpenMP compute backends were added recently, but only the initial OpenCL backend is considered in the remainder of this work.

preceding: CUDA and OpenMP compute backends were added recently, but only the initial OpenCL backend is considered in the remainder of this work.
target: The programming interface is compatible with Boost.uBLAS (http://www.boost.org/libs/numeric/ublas) and allows for simple, high-level access to the vast computing resources available on parallel architectures such as GPUs.
trailing: The library’s primary focus is on common linear algebra operations (BLAS levels 1, 2 and 3) and the solution of large sparse systems of equations by means of iterative methods with optional preconditioners.

preceding: With these considerations in mind, we select a sample of low-redshift galaxies using the SDSS Data Release 7 (DR7; Abazajian et al. 2009), which provides high-fidelity ugriz photometry and spectroscopic redshifts for hundreds of thousands of galaxies in the nearby Universe.

preceding: The 1 ks exposure time cut is the minimum time necessary to obtain an accurate measurement of the total UV flux of extended sources at the typical redshift of galaxies in the SDSS (Wyder et al. 2007).

target: Like all SED-modeling codes designed to extract the physical properties of galaxies (See http://www.sedfitting.org and Walcher et al. trailing: An SSP is an idealized stellar population formed in an instantaneous burst of star formation which evolves passively thereafter with time t.
